title: VideoProductionTermMap
description: Machine-readable version of claude-corrected_term_map.html. Defines production vocabulary along four orthogonal axes: Domain, VocabularyType, DiegeticStatus, PipelineStage. Intended for schema validation, prompt construction, and tooling that needs to resolve or disambiguate film/video production terms.

domain.description: Whether the term belongs to the audio or visual track — mutually exclusive.

vocabulary_type.description: Whether the term describes an aesthetic preference or an operational technique.

diegetic_status.description: Whether the element exists within the story-world (diegetic) or is imposed by the author outside of it (non_diegetic). Can be ambiguous for some terms.

pipeline_stage.description: Which production phase the term primarily belongs to.

overlap_types.physical_co_determination: One physical decision that generates vocabulary in two separate domains (e.g., a lighting decision creates both a light-style entry and a color-style entry).
overlap_types.same_operation_different_stage: The same creative operation can be executed at different pipeline stages (e.g., light FX can be practical on-set or synthetic in post).
overlap_types.discipline_handoff: Two disciplines collaborate without one containing the other (e.g., Motion Graphics FX hands off to VFX for integration, but neither is a subset of the other).

terms.label: Soundtrack
terms.definition: Composed or licensed music that accompanies the visual track. May be diegetic (a band playing in the scene) or non-diegetic (orchestral score heard only by the audience).
terms.overlap_notes: In industrial/compositional work, sound designers and composers share the same session; the boundary between scored music and designed sound events blurs.
corrected_misuses: Do not use 'soundtrack' to refer to all audio in a film — it refers specifically to the music component.
Do not conflate with 'sound design', which covers non-musical audio events.

terms.label: Sound FX (Sound Effects)
terms.definition: Designed audio events tied to on-screen or off-screen actions (footsteps, explosions, ambient noise). Diegetic when representing a real event in the story world; can bleed into non-diegetic when used expressively.
terms.overlap_notes: Industrial/compositional sessions treat sound events and music as a continuum.

terms.label: Visual FX (VFX)
terms.definition: Post-production visual techniques that alter or augment the photographed image: compositing, CGI integration, matte painting, wire removal, digital set extension, de-aging.
terms.overlap_notes: Motion graphics may be handed off to VFX for compositing integration, but motion graphics is not a subset of VFX.
corrected_misuses: VFX ≠ color grading. Color grading is a separate post-production discipline handled by a colorist in a DI session.
VFX ≠ motion graphics. Motion graphics are graphic design in motion; VFX manipulate photographic material.
VFX ≠ practical effects. Practical effects are physical, on-set; VFX are digital, in post.

terms.label: Graphic FX / Motion Graphics
terms.definition: Animated graphical elements that are typically non-diegetic: title cards, lower thirds, kinetic typography, animated overlays, infographic animations, UI representations.
terms.overlap_notes: Graphic FX collaborate with VFX for compositing but are a distinct discipline with different toolchains (After Effects, Motion vs. Nuke, Flame).
corrected_misuses: Graphic FX ≠ a subset of VFX — collaboration is not containment.
Not all on-screen graphics are Graphic FX; diegetic screens (a phone in the scene) may be handled by VFX.

terms.label: Light Style
terms.definition: The Director of Photography's recurring aesthetic decisions about light: hardness (hard vs. soft), directionality (front/side/back/rim), color temperature (warm/cool/neutral), and contrast ratio. Decided in pre-production, executed on set.
terms.overlap_notes: A single on-set lighting decision simultaneously determines the light style and constrains the color style. They are one physical event described by two vocabularies.
corrected_misuses: Light style ≠ light FX. Light style is a pre-production aesthetic decision; light FX are specific visual phenomena (lens flares, god rays) added in production or post.

terms.label: Light FX
terms.definition: Specific light-based visual phenomena: lens flares, god rays, volumetric shafts, bokeh light points, practical light spills. Can be created practically on-set (a rigged practical) or synthetically in post (composited in).
corrected_misuses: Light FX ≠ light style. Light style is the overall aesthetic language; light FX are specific phenomena within it.
terms.pipeline_note: The same light FX (e.g., lens flare) can be a production practical effect or a post-production composite — the operation is stage-ambiguous.

terms.label: Color Style
terms.definition: The overall color palette language of a film: warm/cool balance, hue biases, tonal range (high-key/low-key), palette restrictions. A non-diegetic authorial choice executed in the Digital Intermediate (DI) by the colorist.
terms.overlap_notes: On-set lighting constrains the colorist's palette. Both vocabularies describe consequences of the same physical decisions.
corrected_misuses: Color style ≠ color FX. Color style is the overall palette language; color FX are specific transformations applied within that session.

terms.label: Color FX
terms.definition: Specific color transformations applied during the DI: LUT application, bleach bypass emulation, cross-processing looks, selective hue shifts, vignetting, grain addition. Applied in the same DI session as color style but are distinct, discrete operations.
terms.overlap_notes: Color FX are executed in the DI session, not by VFX artists. They are a separate discipline.
corrected_misuses: Color FX ≠ VFX. Both are post-production but handled by different departments with different toolchains.
Color FX ≠ color style. Style is the overall palette language; FX are specific transformations within a single DI pass.

terms.label: Graphics Style
terms.definition: The overall visual design language applied to graphic elements: line quality, rendering approach (flat/3D/painterly), typographic system, palette logic, icon grammar. Defines consistency rules for all on-screen graphic material.
corrected_misuses: Graphics style is a design system specification, not a synonym for 'animation style' or 'art direction'.
Graphics style applies to graphic elements specifically — not to the overall visual tone of a live-action film (that is color_style + light_style).

terms.label: Graphic Novel
terms.definition: A FORMAT (long-form sequential art in book form), not a visual style. Graphic novels may contain dozens of incompatible visual styles (realistic, cartoonish, abstract, painterly). Citing 'graphic novel' as a style descriptor is a category error.
corrected_misuses: 'Graphic novel style' is not a valid style descriptor — it names a container format, not a visual language.
Specify the actual visual style instead: 'Frank Miller noir ink style', 'Moebius ligne claire', 'watercolour painted panels', etc.

corrected_overlaps.description: One on-set lighting decision simultaneously instantiates a light-style value and constrains color-style space. Both vocabularies describe different consequences of the same physical act. Neither contains the other — they are co-determined.

A lens flare can be a practical effect (rigged on-set during production) or a synthetic composite (added in post). The phenomenon is the same; the pipeline stage is ambiguous until the production plan specifies which approach is used.
Motion graphics are delivered to VFX for compositing integration into the final image. This is a handoff between collaborating disciplines, not containment. Graphic FX is not a subset of VFX.

schema_usage_notes.for_gen_params_prompt_construction: When building prompts for generative-AI tools, use term IDs from this map to annotate which vocabulary a prompt element belongs to. This enables downstream validation that prompts are internally consistent (e.g., not mixing non_diegetic and diegetic descriptors in conflicting ways).
schema_usage_notes.for_cinematic_spec: The CinematicSpec fields lighting_style and lighting_direction in video-project-schema-v2.json correspond to the 'light_style' term here. The vfx_notes field corresponds to 'vfx'. Color grading (color_grade) corresponds to 'color_fx'.
schema_usage_notes.for_animation_taxonomy: See claude-animation-taxonomy-v1.0.md for the formal animation artifact definition. The 'graphics_style' term here maps to the visual_style.iota parameter in that taxonomy.
